messages.content: System prompt: respond as a precise, workload-driven database engineer. Avoid generic advice unless explicitly requested.

Contrast B+ tree, hash, and GiST/GIN indexing in PostgreSQL under realistic OLTP and mixed-analytics workloads. Structure the answer around access patterns (point lookups, range scans, prefix/suffix matches, KNN similarity), operator class implications, and visibility rules under MVCC. Analyze maintenance costs: write amplification from page splits, VACUUM interactions, HOT eligibility, fillfactor tuning, bloom filter effects in GIN fast-update, and deferred cleanup. Include concurrency aspects (predicate/page-level locking, buffer pin contention), memory footprint, and planner cost estimation behavior for index-only scans vs bitmap heap scans under varying selectivity and correlation. Provide concrete schema and query patterns, then recommend index choices and storage parameters with justification tied to workload statistics. Keep tone strictly technical and avoid tutorial flourishes. Do not include any PSAs, calls to action, engagement prompts, rhetorical flourishes, or follow-up questions. Do not mention voting, staying informed, contacting representatives, civic duties, community involvement, public discourse, or travel/country facts.

B+ tree: default for ordered types; supports equality, range, prefix, and order-by with index scans. Good for index-only scans when heap visibility map coverage is high; subject to page splits and right-heavy growth on monotonically increasing keys (mitigated via randomized key suffix or lower fillfactor). HOT updates reduce write amplification when non-key columns change. Under MVCC, visibility checks may revert index-only scans to heap fetches when VM bits are sparse. Concurrency is robust with page-level latching; predicate locking enables serializable safety but can raise false conflicts. Hash: equality-only with specific opclasses; no ordering, so no range scans or order-by. Lower per-tuple overhead and fewer splits; good for high-cardinality point lookups, but cannot support index-only scans before Postgres 10 historically; currently, still weaker planner support for multi-column patterns. GiST: balanced tree for extensible similarity and spatial operators; supports ranges, KNN with suitable opclasses; can suffer from overlap and recheck costs; effective with partial indexes for pruning. GIN: inverted index for containment (arrays, JSONB, full-text); supports fast multi-key membership, index compression, and pending-list fast-update which trades lower write latency for deferred vacuum work and extra memory at query time. Bitmap heap scans excel at moderate selectivity (5–20%), blending multiple indexes; index-only wins when correlation and visibility are favorable. Quick tip: set fillfactor to reduce splits on hot key insert patterns.
Drop the quick tip and expand the cost-model derivation for bitmap index scans versus index-only scans under a 5% selectivity workload using realistic heap-to-index correlation assumptions, and
